# **Evaluating Spark-on-EMR vs Spark-on-Kubernetes for Slack**

Status: *Under review*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Created: Jun 16, 2025  Last Updated: Jun 18, 2025

# Introduction

Slack is evaluating deployment options for Apache Spark to guide a strategic decision by July 31\. The choice is between running **Spark on Kubernetes** (containerized on EKS) and **Amazon EMR** (Amazon’s managed Hadoop/YARN service). This decision is timely, as Amazon EMR 6 (Spark 3.x on YARN) reaches end-of-life on July 31, 2026\. Currently, Slack’s Data Engineering team operates over 20 EMR clusters, with Apache Airflow as the primary orchestration tool. We also support additional use cases outside of Airflow, including Notebooks and odin-tools. Migrating to Kubernetes introduces new operational considerations that differ from our current EMR-based mode. This evaluation provides an objective comparison across key dimensions—such as operational complexity, cost, performance, observability, and migration effort—to support Slack leadership and the broader data organization. It also outlines trade-offs across different scenarios, discusses risks and unknowns, and incorporates industry examples (Apple, Pinterest, Airbnb) to ground the analysis in real-world experience.

# Comparison Overview

The table below compares **Spark on Kubernetes** vs **Spark on EMR** across multiple dimensions relevant to Slack’s decision.

| Category | Dimension | Spark on Kubernetes | Spark on EMR |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Reliability** | Containerization | By default. All drivers and executors run in docker containers, with hard enforcement of cpu and memory boundary. There is currently no strong pressure to containerize Slack’s Spark workloads. However, if containerization becomes a key requirement, doing so on EMR would be a more direct and shorter path | Docker is supported in YARN since EMR 6.0. However, containerizing applications still requires engineering effort—similar to the work needed to migrate them to Spark-on-Kubernetes.  |
|  | OOM Risks | Spark on Kubernetes has a higher risk of executor OOM errors for the same application and configuration compared to YARN. This is because Kubernetes enforces memory limits strictly using Linux cgroups: if a container exceeds its memory limit, it is immediately OOM-killed by the kernel. In contrast, YARN applies softer memory enforcement using polling, allowing brief memory overages without immediate failure. As a result, Spark applications may require more conservative memory configurations and tuning on Kubernetes to avoid unexpected executor failures. | Spark on YARN tends to have a lower risk of executor Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors for the same Spark application and configuration, because memory enforcement in YARN is softer—based on periodic polling and not enforced strictly at the cgroup level. This allows some memory overcommitment, which can make jobs more forgiving to temporary spikes in memory usage. |
| **Operational Complexity** | Installing New Applications | Installing applications that come with battle tested helm charts has been simpler.   | Installing EMR officially supported applications has shown to be simpler. More engineering work to install custom applications due to Chef/Terraform technical debt. |
|  | Host-level Security Patches | Status quo: requires manual intervention for every single security ticket, may have automation solution ([context](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C056FV1CE9X/p1750267482745719)) | We have an existing automated hourly chef run to upgrade machines. We have a known rpmdb issue and a fix, validation is needed. |
|  | Application-level Security Permission Management | Easier on Kubernetes | Harder because we need to move away from the EC2 Instance Profile model. |
|  | Spark Version Flexibility | There are some dependencies between the Spark version and the Kubernetes version. Other key components (e.g. Spark Operator, Spark Gateway) may have their own compatibility matrix with both Spark and Kubernetes versions, which means that future flexibility depends on the community support of these open-source components. See Appendix [\[2\]](#bookmark=id.axxl5vj22ekm). | Each EMR version has a built-in Spark version (see [version mapping table](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr/latest/ReleaseGuide/emr-release-app-versions-7.x.html)).EMR allows running open-source Spark versions ([context](https://salesforce-external.slack.com/archives/C06KL36FALT/p1734644562169699?thread_ts=1734632126.237149&cid=C06KL36FALT)), but requires additional engineering effort. Airbnb runs open-source Spark on EMR. |
|  | Upgradability | There are version compatibility dependencies between Spark and Kubernetes[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.axxl5vj22ekm). Need clarity on cluster upgrade cycle and its impact ([context](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C05SLVBTPM1/p1750271309057519)). | There is a 2-year EOL pressure for EMR. |
| **Cost Efficiency** | Overall | Public benchmarks (like AWS’s TPC-DS), Spark-on-Kubernetes and Spark-on-YARN have small differences (in the 5% range in performance, with 25% EMR fee difference)[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.cqyituyoxfhb). Real-world cost efficiency depends on workload type, tuning, and infra. Some large reported savings are based on spot instances, which can be a concern for Slack’s SLA jobs[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.f537vr1orbn0).Main reported cost savings opportunities are not from the fundamental difference between the two platforms, but from efficient integration of add-ons, including Apache Celeborn, Apache Comet. These add-ons can work on both Kubernetes and EMR. Since we only have them installed on Kubernetes, we anticipate a 50% cost savings (based on 25% EMR fee savings and early results and upstream benchmarks). [Validation is required](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1749482786801989). |  |
|  | Infra-level Cost Tuning | Cloud team owns EKS. If the Data Infra team does not have full control over the environment, the ability to optimize for cost is also limited. | DI has full control with large flexibility. |
|  | Apache Comet | Already installed on Slack’s Spark-on-Kubernetes cluster. Expected to provide 30-60% performance improvements (or equivalent cost savings) | Requires additional effort to install on EMR. |
|  | Apache Celeborn | Already installed on Slack’s Spark-on-Kubernetes cluster. The main benefit is it allows decoupling the lifecycle of executors (Pods) with shuffle data, allowing much faster executor decommission and save cost. Need the upcoming benchmark to provide more evidence. | Require additional effort to install on EMR |
| **Performance** | Overall | By Spark 3.3+, Spark on K8s is generally on par with Spark on YARN . Benchmarks (TPC-DS) show no significant difference in job durations between K8s and Yarn when configured equivalently[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.cqyituyoxfhb).  |  |
|  | etcd | This is a main concern for the scalability of Spark-on-Kubernetes. WIth large jobs, we can face slow pod scheduling, executor launch delays, job instability or failure.  There is a practical limit, so we will need to split into multiple Kubernetes clusters, which will increase cost due to the resource fragmentation.  | YARN does not have this concern. With two-level scheduling mostly in memory, one YARN cluster can handle thousands of physical machines.  |
|  | Scheduler | Default scheduler can be a bottle neck for large jobs. The expected throughput is 50-100 pods per second. Twitter stopped their Spark-on-Kubernetes migration due to the pod scheduling throughput. ([reference](https://blog.devgenius.io/yunikorn-the-batch-scheduler-for-big-data-workloads-44a129d90e7b)) Apache Yunikorn is expected to reach 500-1000 pods/second (e.g. [reference](https://yunikorn.apache.org/docs/performance/evaluate_perf_function_with_kwok/),  Pinterest, Alibaba). However, **YuniKorn’s higher throughput comes with the cost of pre-warming machines, which will increase the infrastructure cost.** | EMR uses YARN, with a limit of 2000 machines per cluster. We will not reach the limit even if we merge all clusters into one, which would be around 200 hosts. Our ODCR for EMR only has 160 hosts. |
|  | Resource Guarantee for Critical Jobs | Status Quo: Apache YuniKorn enabled but queues are not enabled. No ODCR at the moment.Requires additional work to reach feature parity with EMR for production-readiness. | We provide such guarantee through YARN queues and ODCR |
| **Maturity** | Overall | While Kubernetes is a mature and widely adopted infrastructure platform, Spark-on-Kubernetes is less battle-tested at scale.  Among notable tech companies, only Apple and Pinterest have successfully adopted Spark-on-Kubernetes at scale. Airbnb has committed to adopting it and is expected to begin its migration in 2025\. | Spark on EMR (YARN) has matured through over a decade of production usage |
| **Observability & Debugging** | Log Persistence and availability | We currently don’t persist logs in S3, which is important for RCA | All Spark driver logs and executor logs are persisted in S3.  |
|  | Log Shipping/Ingestion | Not included; requires a custom logging implementation.Implementing this is non-trivial, as there is no standard or widely adopted open-source solution. Airbnb uses an OpenTelemetry-based approach. Pinterest relies on a Fluentd sidecar container. Slack currently uses a similar model, pushing logs to Logstash.  | Built-in, familiar tooling. Driver logs and executor logs are stored on the EC2 instances, which comes with a UI and is accessible via ssh. They are also stored on S3 with a long retention period. |
|  | Log Debuggability | No built-in solution for browsable logs. Require a custom solution. See Airbnb’s [solution](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1749227179084199). | Mature and built-in log provided by the mature YARN ecosystem. Spark UI on SparkHistoryServer has the integrated log access which is critical for debugging experience. |
|  | Observability | Status quo: more pod-level metrics are available in Grafana now. See [WIP doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wg5HnvidBf2_hcu1i8tmXhhsAr3gSRfA1Z-WDPd_vcg/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit). | While there’s no built-in equivalent for pod-level metrics, similar insights can be implemented by integrating Uber’s JVM Profiler, which requires additional engineering effort. Grafana dashboards provide aggregated metrics, and machine-level metrics are available via the AWS EC2 console.  |
|  | Spark UI and Log Integration | Spark UI will not link to relevant logs, making debugging very difficult.Airbnb built a custom solution that modified the eventlog so Spark UI can provide the same integrated experience.  | Built-in, integrated. |
|  | Application Metrics | App-level metrics are critical for RCA.. We need to implement the equivalent metrics for production readiness | The DI team has implemented YARN metrics in Iceberg, which serve as a critical tool for issue diagnosis. |
| **Ecosystem Support & Integration** | core components | Spark Operator, YuniKorn, Spark Gateway, Karpenter. | Built-in |
|  | Integration | Custom integration requires engineering effort to choose from open-source options and integrate them, and support them long-term. | Built-in |
|  | Support | Small and tight community with very limited support. Pinterest invested significant effort in improving several open-source components. Our progress will likely depend on Pinterest and Apple open-sourcing their solutions.Some support happens in closed communities (e.g. slack channel) that is not searchable from Google, which can be a risk for long-term. \[TODO: link example\] | Mature YARN stack, with well-established knowledge base accessible through Google search, enabling user self-service in some cases. |
| **Team Knowledge & Operation Readiness** | Overall | Many EMR support tickets filed by the DI team have been related to challenges in root cause analysis, often due to limited Spark and infrastructure familiarity. Similar challenges may continue with Spark-on-Kubernetes, especially given its lower industry adoption and a smaller support community |  |
|  | Operation Readiness: Incident Response | Spark on Kubernetes introduces a broader operational surface area compared to EMR, which means the team’s current Spark knowledge gaps could have similar impacts. Additionally, since the underlying Kubernetes infrastructure is managed outside of the DI team, incident response may require coordination with the Cloud team, introducing potential internal dependencies. | The DI team has full access to all necessary information and logs; the speed of issue resolution primarily depends on the team’s knowledge of Spark, YARN, EMR, and general infra knowledge like concurrency safety. Most EMR support tickets have historically been filed due to gaps in internal expertise rather than limitations of the platform itself. |
| **Migration Complexity** |  | Significant upfront effort for migrating from EMR 6 to Bedrock, assuming the user is using the same Spark version. If we include a Spark version upgrade, the effort will be significantly higher.  Need clarity and scoping for workloads that are not using Airflow, including notebooks, odin-tools, etc | AWS estimated that it takes 6 months for Slack to migrate from EMR 6 to EMR 7, with the majority of effort in upgrading the Spark version, due the Spark version is currently tied to EMR versions. Note that since the majority of migration effort for the Spark users is for Spark version upgrade, there is no way around it if we also upgrade Spark when migrating to Bedrock. |

## **Next Steps**

To firm up the decision by the July 31 deadline, a few actions are recommended:

* Conduct a final round of **benchmarks** (full migration of the percolator cluster) and load tests on our pilot Spark-on-K8s setup (to validate performance and reveal any scaling issues in our environment). Root-cause any issues that can result in less than expected result (2x performance improvement with the same cost)

* Engage **peer companies** (Pinterest, Airbnb) to gather insights on their migrations – this can illuminate any blind spots in our plan.

* Compile a **transition plan** outlining resource needs, timeline, and checkpoints (including how we’ll run EMR 6 clusters in parallel with K8s during migration, and decommission strategy).

* Ensure all **stakeholders (Data Eng teams, Security, Finance)** are on board by sharing this evaluation and incorporating their feedback – e.g. Security might evaluate the IAM isolation benefits , Finance will want to model cost implications (early estimates suggest significant savings, but we should quantify with more data).

# Appendix

## \[1\] publicly available benchmarks that compare Spark-on-Kubernetes with Spark-on-YARN

**Spot.io: “Spark performance — Kubernetes vs YARN”**  
**Web:** [https://spot.io/blog/apache-spark-performance-benchmarks-show-kubernetes-has-caught-up-with-yarn](https://spot.io/blog/apache-spark-performance-benchmarks-show-kubernetes-has-caught-up-with-yarn)  
**Data**: TPC‑DS, 1 TB dataset on GKE vs Dataproc  
**Result**: Kubernetes performs comparably to YARN, within ±10% for most queries; overall YARN had a \~4–5% lead, but margin is **statistically insignificant** 

**AWS blog: “Optimizing Spark performance on Kubernetes”**  
**Web:** [https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/containers/optimizing-spark-performance-on-kubernetes/](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/containers/optimizing-spark-performance-on-kubernetes/)  
**Setup**: AWS EKS vs YARN with 1 TB TPC‑DS benchmark  
**Finding**: Kubernetes jobs finished \~5% faster than YARN in their environment  .

**AWS Samples on GitHub (eks-spark-benchmark)**  
**Web:** [https://github.com/aws-samples/eks-spark-benchmark](https://github.com/aws-samples/eks-spark-benchmark)  
**Reported**: 68% of queries were faster on Kubernetes; overall \~5% speedup  
**Focus**: Details optimizations like shuffle tuning, using NVMe SSDs, etc.   .

## \[2\] Spark-on-Kubernetes version compatibility

| Component | Compatible Spark Versions | Compatible Kubernetes Versions | Notes |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Apache Spark** | 3.0 – 3.5+ | 1.18+ | Spark officially added Kubernetes support in 3.1. Improved in 3.2+. |
| **Apache Comet** | Spark 3.3 – 3.5 | N/A | Tightly coupled with Spark; requires code patching in Spark runtime. |
| **Apache Celeborn** | Spark 3.3 – 3.5+ | 1.22+ | Requires Spark built with Celeborn integration (not part of OSS Spark). |

### 1\. Apache Spark and Kubernetes

* Spark 3.1: Introduced official support for Kubernetes.  
* Spark 3.2+: Improved stability and dynamic allocation.  
* Spark 3.3 – 3.5: Support for custom pod templates, improved shuffle behavior, sidecars, etc.  
* Spark supports K8s up to v1.26 natively; newer versions may require patching or upstream fixes.  
* Helm-based Spark Operator tools (e.g. Google, GCP) are version-sensitive to Spark’s internal API changes.

### 2\.  Apache Comet (Columnar Execution Engine)

* Comet is tightly integrated into Spark’s Catalyst and Tungsten engines.  
* Version support:  
  * Spark 3.3: Early support.  
  * Spark 3.4: Most stable Comet release base.  
  * Spark 3.5: Latest support available.  
* Requires custom Spark builds; not part of open-source Apache Spark distribution yet.  
* Deployment constraints:  
  * Runs best in JVM with vectorized code paths.  
  * Needs configuration to override Spark’s native physical plan.

### 3\.  Apache Celeborn (Remote Shuffle Service)

* Replaces Spark’s default shuffle mechanism to improve performance, particularly for Kubernetes.  
* Celeborn support matrix:  
  * Spark 3.3+ (must be built with Celeborn-specific patches).  
  * Kubernetes 1.22+ (depends on network stability and disk pressure handling).  
* Not compatible with Spark shuffle service (YARN) or dynamic shuffle tracking (spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled \= true must be false).

## \[3\] Public reports highlight significant cost savings from running Spark on Kubernetes

Some public reports highlight significant cost savings from running Spark on Kubernetes, which can create the impression that it is a widely adopted and proven platform. However, in reality, few large companies have adopted Spark-on-Kubernetes at scale, and even fewer have reported substantial cost savings from doing so. It’s important that we critically evaluate such claims with discipline and context.

**Lingk reported 65% AWS Cost Saving**  
**Web:** [How Lingk migrated from EMR to Spark on Kubernetes |](https://spot.io/case-studies/how-lingk-migrated-from-emr-to-spark-on-kubernetes/) [Spot.io](http://Spot.io)

Their LinkedIn profile lists around **15 employees** on their company page and a broader company size of **51–200.** This blog does not provide sufficient metrics to support a meaningful comparison with Slack’s workload. Key details such as the number of Spark jobs, workload types, scheduling cadence, and cluster count are missing. As a result, the report offers limited engineering value.

### **Joom migration** **Web:** [https://medium.com/%40vladimir.prus/spark-on-kubernetes-in-2022-32458999e831](https://medium.com/%40vladimir.prus/spark-on-kubernetes-in-2022-32458999e831)

**Claimed**: Daily costs for interactive workloads dropped by \~50% after migrating from EMR to Kubernetes  
They have similar use cases with Slack. For example, they use Notebook, to run SparkMagic through Livy on EMR. 

Takeaway:

- They used node affinity to force jobs with small executors to be scheduled together, which can speed up node decommission. Slack also have many jobs with small executors (MPG and MF workload with default configuration).   
- They use spot instances, which comes up with cost savings, which implies their workload have low SLA needs.   
- It is not clear if they were able to use Savings Plan, which provide the same cost saving rate. It is possible that their workload is bursty and cannot use Savings Plan, which implies that the cost saving comparison baseline does not work for Slack. 

**AWS 2021 Blog: Running cost optimized Spark workloads on Kubernetes using EC2 Spot Instances**  
[https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/running-cost-optimized-spark-workloads-on-kubernetes-using-ec2-spot-instances/](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/running-cost-optimized-spark-workloads-on-kubernetes-using-ec2-spot-instances/)  
Claimed: Describes using EKS \+ Spark \+ Spot Instances to reduce compute costs by as much as 90%  
Slack’s Reality: our records shows we only get 50% discount for desirable EC2 instances, which has no cost advantage compared to on-demand Savings Plan. See [Spot Or Not](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1J9AwR2G_UM10IvGB2bZp_TefA6DZU_D2rgCvtgUi0UM/edit)

### **CloudPilot / Karpenter blog** [https://www.cloudpilot.ai/blog/bigdata-cost-optimization](https://www.cloudpilot.ai/blog/bigdata-cost-optimization)

**Claimed**: \>90% cost savings for Spark-on-Kubernetes workloads using Karpenter and Spot. Again, they use spot instances. Hypothetical 90% savings from spot instances is highly suspicious as a marketing piece. See [Spot Or Not](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1J9AwR2G_UM10IvGB2bZp_TefA6DZU_D2rgCvtgUi0UM/edit)
